title: Shredded Chicken with Black Bean Sauce

ingredients.title: For the Black Bean Sauce
items: 3 tbsp fermented black beans (douchi), rinsed and roughly chopped
1 tsp cornstarch + 2 tbsp water (slurry for thickening)

instruction.title: Poach and Shred the Chicken
steps: Place chicken breasts in a pot and cover with cold water (or a mixture of water + ice for tender texture).
Add salt, white pepper, and Shaoxing wine.
Bring to a gentle simmer (not rolling boil) over medium heat.
Poach for 10–12 minutes until cooked through (internal temp ~165°F / 74°C).
Remove from water and immediately plunge into ice water to stop cooking.
Drain and shred chicken finely using forks or your hands. Set aside.

instruction.title: Prepare the Black Bean Sauce
steps: Heat vegetable oil in a wok or skillet over medium heat.
Add garlic, ginger, and optional chili; stir-fry until fragrant (~30 seconds).
Add chopped black beans and stir for 1 minute to release aroma.
Pour in chicken stock, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar. Bring to a gentle simmer.
Mix cornstarch with water to create slurry, then slowly add to sauce while stirring. Cook until sauce thickens slightly.

instruction.title: Combine Chicken and Sauce
steps: Add shredded chicken into the wok with sauce.
Toss gently to coat all chicken with the black bean sauce.
Cook for another 1–2 minutes on low heat to allow flavors to meld.
Turn off heat, drizzle with sesame oil, and sprinkle julienned green onions on top.

instruction.title: Presentation
steps: Serve hot, ideally in a shallow bowl.
Garnish with fresh cilantro and additional sliced chili for color and freshness.
Pair with steamed jasmine rice or stir-fried vegetables for a complete meal.

chefTips: Poaching in ice water ensures chicken remains moist and tender.
Rinse fermented black beans to balance saltiness.
Shred finely: thin, uniform pieces absorb sauce better and create elegant texture.
Wok heat control: Keep it medium to avoid burning garlic/ginger but hot enough to bring out aroma.
Balance flavors: Black bean sauce should be savory, slightly sweet, and aromatic—not overpowering.
